elements.sent-2411-001: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have numerous applications across various domains.
elements.sent-2411-002: Several optimization techniques, such as ResNet and SENet, have been proposed to improve model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-003: These techniques improve the model performance by adjusting or calibrating feature responses according to a uniform standard.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, they lack the discriminative calibration for different features, thereby introducing limitations in the model output.
elements.sent-2411-005: Therefore, we propose a method that discriminatively calibrates feature responses.
elements.sent-2411-006: The preliminary experimental results indicate that the neural feature response follows a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-007: Consequently, we compute confidence values by employing the Gaussian probability density function, and then integrate these values with the original response values.
elements.sent-2411-008: The objective of this integration is to improve the feature discriminability of the neural feature response.
elements.sent-2411-009: Based on the calibration values, we propose a plugin-based calibration module incorporated into a modified ResNet architecture, termed Response Calibration Networks (ResCNet).
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive experiments on datasets like CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and ImageNet demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
elements.sent-2411-011: Deep Neural Networks are the prevailing architectures for visual representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-012: The introduction of a wide range of network architectures, including AlexNet~\cite{2012ImageNet , VGG-Net~\cite{2014Very , GoogLeNet~\cite{2014Going , ResNet~\cite{2016Deep , DenseNet~\cite{2017Densely , MobileNet~\cite{howard2017mobilenets , and SqueezeNet~\cite{2016SqueezeNet , {as well as theoretical innovations~\cite{guariglia2018harmonic,guariglia2019primality,guariglia2021fractional , has driven neural networks to achieve impressive performance across various fields, such as image classification~\cite{yang2019hyperspectral,basha2020impact , object recognition\cite{qureshi2022neurocomputing,zhou2024patchdetector , signal decomposition~\cite{mallat1989theory,zheng2019framework , fractal-wavelet modeling~\cite{guido2017effectively,guariglia2016fractional , mathematical equation solving~\cite{MENG2023PINN,raissi2024forward , disaster monitoring~\cite{muhammad2018early,gupta2021deep , and medical image analysis~\cite{yu2021convolutional,niyas2022medical .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The quality of the features extracted by the model directly influences the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, optimizing the model through adjustments to neuron response values is a plausible approach for further improvement.
elements.sent-2411-015: Previous works have also optimized models through feature calibration methods.
elements.sent-2411-016: Such techniques include normalization approaches, like Batch Normalization (BN)~\cite{ioffe2015bn , intermediate network modules, such as Squeeze-and-Excitation Networks (SENet)~\cite{hu2018senet , {and optimizers, such as Lion~\cite{chen2024symbolic and Gradient Search-based Binary Runge Kutta Optimizer (GBRUN)~\cite{dou2024gbrun .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: These methods have enhanced model performance by refining features through channel and spatial attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-018: While these methods have achieved notable improvements, they primarily focus on scaling raw feature values without sufficiently addressing feature distinctiveness.
elements.sent-2411-019: This method diverges from existing techniques by emphasizing the optimization of feature distribution and enhancing their distinctiveness rather than just scaling feature values.
elements.sent-2411-020: By focusing on feature distribution and distinctiveness, our approach offers a novel contribution to the field.
elements.sent-2411-021: Specifically, we propose a response value calibration technique that introduces additional calibration values to features extracted by deep learning models, thereby enhancing their discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-022: These calibration values essentially compute the confidence of the original features based on the integral of the Gaussian probability density function.
elements.sent-2411-023: More precisely, we fit each neuron with mean and variance parameters to model the Gaussian distribution that the neuron's responses follow.
elements.sent-2411-024: Using the probability density function of this distribution, we calculate a confidence value for each response: the confidence for a response value below the mean corresponds to the integral of the probability density function up to that value, while the confidence for a response above the mean is given by the integral from the symmetrical point of that value with respect to the mean.
elements.sent-2411-025: Based on this response value calibration scheme, we introduce the Response Calibration Layer (RC Layer), similar to the SE layer.
elements.sent-2411-026: Models integrated with this calibration layer are termed Response Calibration Networks (ResCNet).
elements.sent-2411-027: In summary, the main contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-028: Introducing a novel approach to calibrate neuron response values from a perspective of discriminative distribution correction.
elements.sent-2411-029: A plug-in-based calibration module is devised.
elements.sent-2411-030: Based on this plugin, we developed a new model architecture called ResCNet.
elements.sent-2411-031: This branch offers supplementary calibration values for the model’s features.
elements.sent-2411-032: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and ImageNet datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
elements.sent-2411-033: The calibrated model not only enhances classification performance but also extracts more discriminative features.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics{first_pic.pdf} \caption{ Module architecture. From the module architecture, it can be seen that SENet further scales the features extracted by the ResNet residual branches, whereas our proposed architecture provides additional calibration of these features. } \label{fig:first_pic}
elements.sent-2411-034: Recently, significant progress has been made in adjusting the response values of neurons in deep learning models to enhance the performance of various visual tasks.
elements.sent-2411-035: These methods include regularization techniques~\cite{ioffe2015bn,ba2016ln analogous to standardization operations in traditional feature engineering, activation functions~\cite{howard2017mobilenets,hendrycks2016gaussian that introduce nonlinear transformations to enable neural networks to learn and represent complex nonlinear relationships, and various intermediate modules~\cite{hu2018senet,li2019sknet based on attention mechanisms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Furthermore, nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithms~\cite{erdacs2023optimum,sait2024optimal are gaining popularity due to their ease of application and their ability to avoid local optimum points.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this section, we briefly present the feature response adjustment techniques related to this work, which are primarily categorized into regularization methods, activation functions, and attention mechanisms.. Regularization Methods.
elements.sent-2411-038: Training deep neural networks is challenging since the distribution of each layer’s inputs will change with the gradually optimized parameters of the previous layers.
elements.sent-2411-039: This phenomenon is termed internal covariate shift~\cite{ioffe2015bn .}
elements.sent-2411-040: These issues can result in a slower convergence rate for convolutional neural networks when aiming for the optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-041: To alleviate this dilemma, various regularization methods have been proposed, such as Batch Normalization~\cite{ioffe2015bn , Layer Normalization (LN)~\cite{ba2016ln , Group Normalization (GN)~\cite{wu2018gn , and Instance Normalization (IN)~\cite{ulyanov2016instance .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Normalization techniques, by adjusting the responses of neurons, accelerate the model's convergence speed.
elements.sent-2411-043: Batch Normalization depends on the batch size, whereas Layer Normalization, Group Normalization, and Instance Normalization do not depend on the batch size.
elements.sent-2411-044: Currently, the primary function of normalization layers is to standardize the outputs of the preceding layer, but this process does not deeply consider the potential significance of feature values.
elements.sent-2411-045: This work focuses on exploring how to measure and utilize the importance of feature values more thoroughly from the perspective of response distribution, thereby advancing model optimization.. Activation Functions.
elements.sent-2411-046: Activation functions introduce non-linearity to models by adjusting feature responses, including ReLU, ReLU6~\cite{howard2017mobilenets , GELU~\cite{hendrycks2016gaussian , Tanh, and Sigmoid.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Specifically, ReLU filters out values that are less than 0.
elements.sent-2411-048: ReLU6, introduced in MobileNets~\cite{howard2017mobilenets , is essentially a modification of the rectified linear unit that limits the activation to a maximum size of 6, enhancing the robustness of low-precision computations.}
elements.sent-2411-049: GELU adjusts neuron responses from the perspective of mathematical expectation.
elements.sent-2411-050: Additionally, Tanh and Sigmoid map the activation values to specific intervals.
elements.sent-2411-051: These approaches do not take into account the distribution characteristics of the feature values, thereby overlooking their potential positive impact.
elements.sent-2411-052: In contrast, this work aims to assess the importance of the original feature values more precisely from the perspective of their distribution, seeking to achieve a more precise quantification and utilization of features.. Attention Mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-053: There have been several articles on attention mechanisms, such as SENet~\cite{hu2018senet , SKNet~\cite{li2019sknet , CBAM~\cite{woo2018cbam , {Involution~\cite{li2021involution , and Halo~\cite{vaswani2021scaling .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: SENet {and Involution intrinsically introduce dynamics conditioned on the input, which can be regarded as a self-attention function on channels.}
elements.sent-2411-055: SKNet employs convolution kernels of different sizes and a method similar to SENet to dynamically calculate the weight of each convolution kernel.
elements.sent-2411-056: CBAM {and Halo combine channel attention and spatial attention, concatenating them to determine the weight of each feature map.}
elements.sent-2411-057: These articles primarily focus on learning a weight at each level of the feature map through the use of attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-058: This work evaluates the importance of the original feature values more meticulously from the perspective of their distribution and introduces additional adjustment values for the original features.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig_violinplot.pdf} \caption{ The distribution of response values after model convergence using ResNet-32 on CIFAR-100 dataset. From the outer contour of the violin plot, it can be observed that the response values of a single convolutional kernel (neuron) roughly follow a Gaussian distribution. } \label{fig:res32_violin}
elements.sent-2411-059: We first obtained the features of the samples after passing through the feature extraction layer and then performed a global average pooling (GAP) operation to obtain the final feature values.
elements.sent-2411-060: The dimension of these feature values corresponds to the number of output channels from the last convolutional layer.
elements.sent-2411-061: For the entire dataset, we obtained a final data dimension of [N, C] (N is the sample size of the entire dataset, and C refers to the number of convolutional kernels in the final convolutional layer).
elements.sent-2411-062: Specifically, we use the CIFAR-100 dataset~\cite{krizhevsky2009learning , and the model employed is ResNet-32.}
elements.sent-2411-063: We visualized the feature values, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:res32_violin .}
elements.sent-2411-064: From the visualized results, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:res32_violin , it can be observed that the response values of each convolutional kernel approximately follow a Gaussian distribution, with different kernels adhering to different Gaussian distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Specifically, in deep learning models, a neuron's output is typically a weighted sum of multiple input features.
elements.sent-2411-066: The Central Limit Theorem states that when the number of independent random variables is sufficiently large (greater than $30$), the distribution of their sum will approximate a Gaussian distribution, even if the random variables themselves are not Gaussian distributed.
elements.sent-2411-067: According to the Central Limit Theorem, this output distribution is likely to approximate a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-068: Furthermore, in modern network architectures, intermediate modules like BN, LN, and activation functions such as GELU are predicated on the assumption that feature values follow a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-069: The encoder in Variational Auto-Encoders (VAE)~\cite{kingma2013auto also relies on this assumption.}
elements.sent-2411-070: By integrating experimental observations with prior research, and under the assumption that features follow a Gaussian distribution, we developed techniques for adjusting response values and implemented them in a specialized network module.
elements.sent-2411-071: In this section, we introduce the core technique of this paper: adjusting the original feature values based on the assumption that they follow a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-072: Specifically, we design two trainable parameters, mean and standard deviation, for each convolutional kernel to fit its corresponding Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-073: Then we use the integral of the Gaussian probability density function corresponding to this distribution to measure the neuron response values, thus obtaining the final calibration values.
elements.sent-2411-074: The calibration feature values are incorporated into the original feature values during the model's training process.
elements.sent-2411-075: Based on this response value calibration method, we propose a plugin-based module to integrate the calibration values into mainstream neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-076: The plugin-based module is incorporated into a modified ResNet architecture, which we call Response Calibration Networks.
elements.sent-2411-077: In the proposed method, we consider the Gaussian distribution's mean to reflect the convolutional kernel's general capability in processing features, which is of significant importance.
elements.sent-2411-078: Response values closer to the mean are assigned larger weights, whereas response values farther from the mean are assigned smaller weights.
elements.sent-2411-079: It is hypothesized that the response values generated by any convolutional kernel follow a Gaussian distribution $\mathbf{E_{k \sim \mathcal{N (\mathbf{u_{k , \boldsymbol{\sigma_{k ^{2 )$, where $ \mathbf{u_{k $ and $ \boldsymbol{\sigma_{k $ are the mean and standard deviation, respectively, of the convolution kernel $k$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: For an response value $ a_{k \leq \mu_{k $, the corresponding weight $ w_{k $ is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}w_{k} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{k} \sqrt{2 \pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{x} \exp \left(-\frac{(t-\mu_{k})^{2}}{2 \sigma_{k}^{2}}\right) dt.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The above results can be simplified with the Error function (erf): \begin{equation}w_{k} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1+\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{a_{k}-\mu_{k}}{\sigma_{k} \sqrt{2}}\right)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: The formula can be further simplified as follows: \begin{equation}w_{k} = \Phi(x) \nonumber =\frac{1}{2}\left[1+\operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right],\end{equation} where $ x = (a_{k - \mu_{k ) / \sigma_{k $ and $\Phi(x) = P(X\le x), X\sim \mathcal{N (0,1)$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal distribution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: This operation ensures that the closer the response value is to the mean, the greater the corresponding weight.
elements.sent-2411-084: If $ a_{k > \mu_{k $, signifying that the response value is distant from the mean, the weight value $w_{k = 1 - \Phi(x) $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In other words, we use the cumulative distribution function at the point symmetric to the mean position as the final weight.
elements.sent-2411-086: This operation reduces the weight associated with a response value as it deviates further from the mean.
elements.sent-2411-087: To summarize, the weight $ w_{k $ is defined by the following expression: \begin{equation}\label{eq:weight_final_eq_v2} w_{k} = \begin{cases} 1- \Phi(x), a_{k} > \mu_{k} \Phi(x), a_{k} \le \mu_{k} \end{cases}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The adjustment value $c_{k}$ is computed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:adjustment_value} c_{k} = a_{k} * w_{k} \nonumber =  a_{k} * (1- \Phi(x)), a_{k} > \mu_{k} a_{k} * \Phi(x), a_{k} \le \mu_{k} .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:adjustment_value} c_{k} = a_{k} * w_{k} \nonumber =  a_{k} * (1- \Phi(x)), a_{k} > \mu_{k} a_{k} * \Phi(x), a_{k} \le \mu_{k} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: From Eq.~\ref{eq:weight_final_eq_v2 , it can be observed that when the neuron response value is less than the mean, our weight formula aligns with that of GELU~\cite{hendrycks2016gaussian .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Conversely, when the neuron's response value exceeds the mean, our weight values differ from GELU's; however, their total remains 1.
elements.sent-2411-091: We apply the adjustment values sequentially before the original activation function.
elements.sent-2411-092: Assuming the activation function is ReLU, the adjustment values are incorporated into its input.
elements.sent-2411-093: Taking the basic block of a ResNet as an example, the input values enter the basic block and split into two branches: the residual branch and the shortcut branch.
elements.sent-2411-094: The residual branch processes the input in the following order: Conv $\rightarrow$ BN $\rightarrow$ ReLU $\rightarrow$ Conv $\rightarrow$ BN.
elements.sent-2411-095: The output of the residual branch is then added to the shortcut branch and passed through a ReLU function.
elements.sent-2411-096: We integrate the proposed method (similar to the SE Layer) after the second BN layer.
elements.sent-2411-097: By examining the feature processing flow, we can observe the difference between our method and SE layer.
elements.sent-2411-098: SE layer scales the original features and uses them as the final output of the residual branch.
elements.sent-2411-099: In contrast, the proposed method adds the calibration values to the original feature values.
elements.sent-2411-100: We have developed this scheme as a module, facilitating its integration into mainstream models.
elements.sent-2411-101: For instance, we have integrated the calibration module into ResNet, naming this model variant Response Calibration Networks (ResCNets).
elements.sent-2411-102: A basic block design is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:rcnet .}
elements.sent-2411-103: We employ Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) to fit the mean and standard deviation corresponding to each convolutional kernel.
elements.sent-2411-104: Subsequently, we use Eq.~\ref{eq:adjustment_value to scale the original feature values, resulting in the calibration values.}
elements.sent-2411-105: In this section, we conducted extensive experiments to validate the effectiveness of the method proposed in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-106: We compared the performance of the original ResNet models with other variants, such as SENet, the ResCNet proposed in this paper across various datasets.
elements.sent-2411-107: Additionally, in the ablation study section, we also explored other integration methods for calibration values.
elements.sent-2411-108: The datasets we adopted contain CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100~\cite{krizhevsky2009learning , SVHN~\cite{netzer2011reading , and ImageNet~\cite{deng2009imagenet , which are widely used datasets for the classification task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Both of the CIFAR datasets~\cite{krizhevsky2009learning consists of 60,000 colour images of size $32 \times 32$ pixels.}
elements.sent-2411-110: CIFAR-10 has 10 distinct classes, and CIFAR-100 contains 100 classes.
elements.sent-2411-111: Each dataset is split into a training set with 50,000 images and a test set with 10,000 images.
elements.sent-2411-112: The Street View House Numbers (SVHN) dataset~\cite{netzer2011reading contains a total of 630,420 color images with a resolution of $32 \times 32$ pixels.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Each image is centered about a number from one to ten.
elements.sent-2411-114: The official dataset split contains 73,257 training images and 26,032 test images, but there are also 531,131 additional training images available.
elements.sent-2411-115: ImageNet is a vast image database widely used in visual object recognition research.
elements.sent-2411-116: The most highly-used subset of ImageNet is the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) 2012 image classification dataset.
elements.sent-2411-117: This dataset spans $1,000$ object classes and contains $1,281,167$ training images and $50,000$ validation images.
elements.figure-2411-004: \textwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{@{}l|cc|cc|ccc@{}} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Params (K)} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{FLOPs (M)} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Accuracy(\%)} Dataset CIFAR-10 \& SVHN CIFAR-100 CIFAR-10 \& SVHN CIFAR-100 CIFAR-10 CIFAR-100 SVHN ResNet-32 466.91 {472.76} {70.37} 70.38 93.72 71.91 95.82 SENet-32 480.35 {486.20} {70.67} 70.68 93.73 72.26 95.77 ResCNet-32 (Ours) {488.33} {494.18} {70.68} 70.68 93.79 72.76 95.87 ResNet-56 855.77 {861.62} {127.91} 127.92 94.51 73.79 96.00 SENet-56 879.96 {885.81} {128.45} 128.46 94.51 74.68 95.88 ResCNet-56 (Ours) 894.33 {900.18} {128.47} 128.47 94.61 73.81 96.10 ResNet-50 23520.84 {23705.25} {1304.94} {1308.84} 95.81 80.79 96.29 SENet-50 26078.39 {26262.80} {1314.48} {1321.98} 95.75 80.68 96.26 ResCNet-50 (Ours) 27324.41 {27508.82} {1316.25} {1316.43} 95.83 80.87 96.31 \end{tabular}} \caption{ Results of the sequential scheme (ResCNet). Model architectures with 32, and 56 layers have 3 stage blocks (downsampling in the last two stages), and 50 layers has 4 stage blocks (downsampling in the last three stages). We compare the performance of ResNet, SENet, and ResCNet models with the same number of layers. The bold indicates the best performance.} \label{tab:tab_rcnet}
elements.sent-2411-118: Our study trained convolutional neural networks for 200 epochs on datasets including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN using the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-119: For the ImageNet dataset, training was conducted for 90 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-120: During the initial phase, models were warmed up for 5 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-121: The learning rates were set as follows: 0.1 for CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet; 0.01 for SVHN.
elements.sent-2411-122: The batch sizes were 128 for CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN, and 256 for ImageNet.
elements.sent-2411-123: In terms of learning rate schedulers, aside from using the StepLR scheduler for the ImageNet dataset, all other datasets employ a CosineAnnealingLR~\cite{loshchilov2016sgdr scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Regarding the reduction hyperparameter for the sequential scheme, a value of 4 was used for ResNet architectures with 32 and 56 layers, while a value of 16 was applied to ResNet with 18, 34, and 50 layers.
elements.sent-2411-125: We compared the performance of the original ResNet models with other variants, including SENet and the ResCNet proposed in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-126: The results on the CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN datasets are shown in Tab.\ref{tab:tab_rcnet .}
elements.sent-2411-127: It can be observed that as the depth of the model increases, the proposed ResCNet outperforms both ResNet and SENet in terms of performance.
elements.sent-2411-128: In scenarios with a larger number of model parameters and deeper layers, the proposed ResCNet performs better than both SENet and ResNet.
elements.figure-2411-005: \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|cccc} Params (M) FLOPs (G) Top-1 Accuracy (\%) Top-5 Accuracy (\%) ResNet-18 (TorchVision)$^*$ 11.70 1.81 69.758 89.078 ResNet-18 (Re-Implementation) 11.70 1.81 70.098 89.260 ResCNet-18 (Ours) 11.82 1.83 70.354 89.586 ResNet-34 (TorchVision)$^*$ 21.80 3.66 73.314 91.42 ResNet-34 (Re-Implementation) 21.80 3.66 73.486 91.526 ResCNet-34 (Ours) 22.04 3.68 73.816 91.546 ResNet-50 (TorchVision)$^*$ 25.60 4.09 76.130 92.862 ResNet-50 (Re-Implementation) 25.60 4.09 76.200 92.886 SENet-50$^*$ - - 76.710 93.380 ResCNet-50 (Ours) 29.36 4.15 76.920 {93.298} \end{tabular}} \caption{ ImageNet result using ResNet and ResCNet. ``$*$'' indicates that the result is from the original paper or the official result. ResNet and ResCNet were trained for 90 epochs, whereas SENet was trained for 100 epochs. The bold indicates the best performance. } \label{tab:imagenet_result}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig_model_acc.pdf} \caption{ Top-1 error of ImageNet using ResNet and ResCNet. From the training curves, it can be observed that the error rate of the ResCNet model is lower than that of the ResNet model. } \label{fig:fig_err_imagenet}
elements.sent-2411-129: We also validated the effectiveness of the ResCNet model on the ImageNet dataset, with the experimental results shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:imagenet_result and Fig.~\ref{fig:fig_err_imagenet .}}
elements.sent-2411-130: It can be observed that ResCNet effectively enhances the performance of the model.
elements.sent-2411-131: Although SENet-50 was trained for 100 epochs, ResCNet-50 outperformed SENet-50 after training for just 90 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-132: The training curves in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig_err_imagenet demonstrate that the Top-1 error rate of the ResCNet model consistently remains below that of the ResNet model throughout the training process, indicating that the ResCNet model has better convergence capabilities.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig_ori_vs_ours_resnet32_violinplot.pdf} \caption{ Comparison of response value distributions. We compared the response value distributions of the ResNet-32, SENet-32, and ResCNet-32 models on the CIFAR-100 dataset. The red dashed bounding boxes serve as visual indicators of the enhanced discriminability in neural feature responses. } \label{fig:ori_vs_ours_resnet32_violinplot}
elements.sent-2411-133: We visualized the calibrated features and compared them with those of ResNet and SENet, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:ori_vs_ours_resnet32_violinplot .}
elements.sent-2411-134: It can be observed that the features generated by the method proposed in this paper exhibit stronger distinctiveness compared to other methods.
elements.sent-2411-135: Although the SE layer can also adjust feature values, the extent of these adjustments is relatively small, and the distinction between different features is not pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-136: Essentially, the SE layer primarily scales the features from the residual branch.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, the approach proposed in this paper involves calculating additional calibration values for the features in the residual branch, which enhances the specificity among features, thereby providing stronger feature distinctiveness.. {.}
elements.sent-2411-138: In this section, we explore the performance limits of the ResCNet model by employing several deep-learning tricks.
elements.sent-2411-139: We conducted experiments using the ResNet-50 and ResCNet-50 models on the CIFAR-100 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-140: The tricks used are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-141: Utilized the downsampling structure proposed by ResNet-D~\cite{he2019bag .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Increased training epochs to 300 and warm-up epochs to 20.
elements.sent-2411-147: Used a larger batch size of 256 without changing the learning rate.
elements.sent-2411-148: Distributed training (2 $\times$ RTX3090, the batch size of each GPU is 128).
elements.figure-2411-008: {\begin{tabular}{@{}l|cc@{}} ResNet-50 ResCNet-50 Baseline 80.79 80.87 +ResNet-D's Downsampling Block 80.91 81.91 +Long Training 82.19 82.21 +AutoAugment 83.14 82.91 +Random Erasing 83.58 83.25 +Mixup 84.60 84.31 +Cutmix 85.81 85.73 +Large BatchSize 85.84 {86.17} +Distributed training 85.92 86.31 \end{tabular}} \caption{ CIFAR-100 result using ResNet-50 and ResCNet-50. All scores are denoted in \%. } \label{tab:more_cifar100_result}
elements.sent-2411-149: We also used a technique similar to Random Erasing, known as Cutout~\cite{devries2017improved , in our preliminary experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-150: However, we found that Random Erasing yielded better results, approximately 0.4\% higher than Cutout.
elements.sent-2411-151: Learning rate scaling did not yield good results.
elements.sent-2411-152: For instance, when the batch size was increased to 256 and the learning rate was adjusted to 0.2, the performance decreased by 0.3\%.
elements.sent-2411-153: Overall, the hyperparameters we used were identified as optimal through extensive experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-154: The experimental results are shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:more_cifar100_result .}
elements.sent-2411-155: The experimental results show that the ResCNet-50 model performs excellently on the CIFAR-100 dataset by incorporating various deep-learning training tricks.
elements.sent-2411-156: Compared to ResNet-50 ($85.92\%$), our proposed ResCNet-50 model achieves a superior performance of $86.31\%$.
elements.sent-2411-157: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve over $86\%$ accuracy on the CIFAR-100 dataset with a 50-layer ResNet model variant without utilizing additional training data or pre-trained models\footnote{https://paperswithcode.com/sota/image-classification-on-cifar-100 , as of the time of submission.}
elements.sent-2411-158: These results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.
elements.sent-2411-159: In this section, we primarily explore the performance differences resulting from the implementation of the RC Layer, as well as the impact of other calibration value integration methods on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-160: In the specific implementation of the RC Layer, we employ three fully connected (FC) layers to fit the mean and standard deviation corresponding to each convolutional kernel.
elements.sent-2411-161: In principle, using two FC layers is sufficient for learning the mean and standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-162: However, employing two FC layers would significantly increase the model's parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-163: For instance, if the convolutional layer has $C$ channels, using two FC layers would increase the model's parameters by $2*C*C$.
elements.sent-2411-164: Conversely, with three FC layers, where the reduction factor in the middle layer is $r$, the total parameter count becomes $3*C*r$.
elements.sent-2411-165: Typically, $r$ is much smaller than $C$, thereby reducing the significant increase in model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-166: Through this approach, we achieve feature calibration without a notable increase in the model's parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-167: We compared the effects of different implementations of the RC Layer on the model.
elements.sent-2411-168: The model utilized was ResCNet-32, and the dataset employed was CIFAR-100.
elements.sent-2411-169: In the experiments, we observed that implementations with two FC layers and three FC layers produced similar results in terms of Top-1 accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-170: Consequently, we also present the Top-5 accuracy to offer a more precise comparison of model performance.
elements.sent-2411-171: We also examined the impact of incorporating an activation function in the middle layer of the three FC layers implementation.
elements.sent-2411-172: The experimental results are shown in Tab.\ref{tab:tab_rescnet32_fc .}
elements.figure-2411-009: \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}ccccc@{}} \#FC reduction Activation layer Top-1 Accuracy (\%) Top-5 Accuracy (\%) 2 - - 72.76 92.67 3 2 - 72.21 92.54 3 4 - 72.76 92.54 3 4 ReLU 72.36 91.96 3 4 Sigmoid 72.38 92.62 \end{tabular} } \caption{ The impact of different implementations of the RC Layer on model performance. The dataset used is CIFAR-100, and the model is ResCNet-32. The first row of experimental results serves as the baseline. The bold indicates the best performance. } \label{tab:tab_rescnet32_fc}
elements.sent-2411-173: Experimental results show minimal differences in final model performance between implementations with two and three FC layers.
elements.sent-2411-174: Adjusting the reduction hyperparameter allows both implementations to achieve comparable performance levels.
elements.sent-2411-175: Given that the module proposed in this paper is an additional component designed not to increase the model's parameter count significantly, we consider these experimental results acceptable.
elements.sent-2411-176: Experimental results also indicate that using an activation function does not enhance model performance.
elements.sent-2411-177: We speculate that this may be because the activation function disrupts the module's learning process for the mean and variance of each neuron, despite potentially providing more nonlinear combinations.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{fig_integration_scheme.pdf} \caption{ Proposed integration schemes. Sequential and parallel refer to the position of the calibration layer relative to the activation function layer. The original approach (ResCNet) is termed ``serial scheme''. } \label{fig:integration_schemes}
elements.sent-2411-178: In integrating the RC Layer, we position the calibration layer after the last BN in the ResNet block, just before the ReLU activation.
elements.sent-2411-179: We also experimented with placing the calibration layer alongside the ReLU and simplified the assumption of a Gaussian distribution to observe the resulting performance differences.
elements.sent-2411-180: In other words, a duplicate of the input to the ReLU function is also passed to the adjustment calculation unit.
elements.sent-2411-181: Logically, both the adjustment calculation unit and the ReLU calculation unit receive the same input and compute in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-182: We refer to this approach as parallel integration, while the original approach is termed serial integration.
elements.sent-2411-183: The diagrams for the two integration methods are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:integration_schemes .}
elements.sent-2411-184: In the parallel scheme, we assume that the input features follow a standard Gaussian distribution with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1.
elements.sent-2411-185: Additionally, we integrate this parallel computation into a design that resembles an activation function similar to ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-186: We name the activation function proposed in this paper as the Gaussian Calibration Linear Unit (GCLU).
elements.sent-2411-187: In this case, the final activation value is the sum of the original response value and the calibration value.
elements.sent-2411-189: The final response value is $f(a_{k )$, the formulaic expression is: \begin{equation}\label{eq:final_act_value} f(a_{k}) = ReLU(a_{k}) + c_{k} \nonumber = ReLU(a_{k}) + a_{k} * w_{k} \nonumber =  a_{k} * (2- \Phi(a_{k})), a_{k} > 0 a_{k} * \Phi(a_{k}), a_{k} \le 0 .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:final_act_value} f(a_{k}) = ReLU(a_{k}) + c_{k} \nonumber = ReLU(a_{k}) + a_{k} * w_{k} \nonumber =  a_{k} * (2- \Phi(a_{k})), a_{k} > 0 a_{k} * \Phi(a_{k}), a_{k} \le 0 .\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-011: \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}c|ccc|ccc@{}} Dataset \multicolumn{3}{c|}{CIFAR-10} \multicolumn{3}{c}{CIFAR-100} Activation layer ReLU GELU GCLU (Ours) ReLU GELU GCLU (Ours) AlexNet 85.85 84.86 86.98 57.65 1.23 62.33 VGG-16 93.86 93.69 94.15 73.21 72.47 74.13 ResNet-8 88.14 88.37 88.38 60.73 60.61 60.82 ResNet-32 93.72 93.73 93.24 71.91 71.87 71.75 ViT-Tiny 82.37 83.12 {82.61} 55.59 57.81 {56.63} Swin-Tiny 85.93 84.76 {85.71} 59.54 57.25 {58.92} Cait-XXS 84.28 84.14 84.46 59.16 59.77 {59.67} \end{tabular}} \caption{ Accuracy performance of different activation functions. The bold indicates the best performance. All scores are denoted in \%. } \label{tab:tab_act_result}
elements.sent-2411-190: Considering the generality of the activation function, we explored the performance of the parallel integration approach across more models utilizing the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-191: The evaluated model architectures include AlexNet, VGGNet, ResNet, Vision Transformer (ViT)~\cite{dosovitskiy2021vit , Swin Transformer~\cite{liu2021swin , and Cait~\cite{touvron2021going .}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: The results are presented in Tab.~\ref{tab:tab_act_result .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Experimental results indicate that the proposed activation function exhibits strong competitiveness relative to other mainstream activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-194: Additionally, issues with model non-convergence were observed when using AlexNet with GELU on the CIFAR-100 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-195: In contrast, the proposed method, similar to ReLU, did not encounter any issues with non-convergence.
elements.sent-2411-196: The results also demonstrate that the proposed activation function significantly enhances performance in models lacking Batch Normalization layers, such as AlexNet.
elements.sent-2411-197: As network depth increases and Batch Normalization layers are introduced, the proposed activation function performs comparably to, or better than, other activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-198: Overall, the activation function proposed in this paper demonstrates significant advantages in shallow convolutional neural network models and approaches to the performance of ReLU in deeper models, or GELU in visual transformers.
elements.figure-2411-012: \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}c|cc|cc@{}} \multirow{2}{*}{Approximation form} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{CIFAR-10} \multicolumn{2}{c}{CIFAR-100} \multicolumn{1}{l}{Accuracy (\%) } Time (hour) \multicolumn{1}{l}{Accuracy (\%)} Time (hour) - 93.24 2.73 71.75 2.90 Sigmoid 93.35 2.43 72.32 2.40 Tanh 93.57 2.67 71.67 2.73 \end{tabular} } \caption{ The influence of GCLU's approximation forms on ResNet-32's performance across CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets. "Sigmoid" represents the approximate form of Eq.~\ref{eq:sigmoid_approx}, and "Tanh" represents the approximate form of Eq.~\ref{eq:tanh_approx}. The first row of the experimental results represents the original results without using the approximate forms. The bold indicates the best performance. } \label{tab:tab_cdf_approx}
elements.sent-2411-199: Previous studies~\cite{hendrycks2016gaussian,page1977approximations,bowling2009logistic have pointed out that there are two main approximation forms of the CDF of the standard normal distribution, namely: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sigmoid_approx} \Phi(x)\approx \sigma(1.702 x).\end{equation}. and \begin{equation}\label{eq:tanh_approx} \Phi(x)\approx \frac{1}{2} \left[1 + \tanh\left(\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\left(x + 0.044715 x^3\right)\right)\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-200: We explored the impact of these two approximation forms on model performance using the ResNet-32 model on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-201: The experimental results are presented in Tab.~\ref{tab:tab_cdf_approx .}
elements.sent-2411-202: The experimental results show that the approximation forms of the function have a relatively minor impact on model performance, and the approximations can accelerate the training speed of the model in practice.
elements.sent-2411-203: The approximate form of Eq.~\ref{eq:sigmoid_approx has the shortest training time because it does not involve complex exponential, division, or square root operations.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Overall, the approximate forms of the GCLU activation function can reduce computational complexity, which is significant for promoting its application in mainstream neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-205: In this paper, we propose a method to adjust neuron responses from a feature distribution perspective.
elements.sent-2411-206: This method involves adapting two trainable parameters, the mean and standard deviation for each neuron, which are used to calculate calibration values for their responses.
elements.sent-2411-207: These calibration values are subsequently added to the original responses.
elements.sent-2411-208: Based on these calibration values, we propose a plugin-based calibration module.
elements.sent-2411-209: This module is integrated into a modified ResNet architecture, termed Response Calibration Networks (ResCNet).
elements.sent-2411-210: Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed response calibration technique.
elements.sent-2411-211: We hope this work will inspire future research on optimizing models from a distributional perspective.
elements.sent-2411-212: This work is supported by Ningbo Natural Science Foundation (2022J182) and Zhejiang Province ``Pioneering Soldier" and ``Leading Goose" R\&D Project under Grant 2023C01027.. \bibliographystyle{elsarticle-num.}
